steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks what the text "most strongly suggests" about how nonvisible light analysis can inform attribution judgments. The passage gives two examples: (1) Monet's Wisteria revealed a hidden earlier step in his process; (2) detecting titanium oxide can expose forgeries. The question links both to the attribution question.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — It can provide new information about the attributed artist's practices.: The Monet example reveals a hidden step in Monet's process, but the question asks specifically about judging attribution. A hidden working method alone does not determine whether an attribution is valid. ✗
  • Choice B — It can definitively undermine that attribution.: The titanium oxide example shows that nonvisible spectra can reveal an anachronistic pigment throughout a painting's layers, proving that a work attributed to an 18th-century artist can only be a forgery. That definitively undermines the attribution in that case. ✓
  • Choice C — It can confirm that attribution with a high degree of confidence.: The passage describes detecting forgeries, not confirming an attribution. ✗
  • Choice D — It can show how the work is similar to others attributed to the same artist.: The passage never discusses comparing multiple works by the same artist. Not supported. ✗
B is correct.
